topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The plastic wrapping felt cheap against my fingertips as I unfolded the bouquet. Bright, aggressively cheerful sunflowers, a shocking shade of yellow that seemed to vibrate with misplaced enthusiasm. There was no note. Not a single scrap of paper, no delicate script to explain the gesture, no whispered sentiment to soften the bluntness of the offering. I stared at the arrangement, my jaw tightening until my molars ached. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air. "What in the world?" I muttered, the words tasting like ash. I looked around the room, as if expecting a hidden camera or a prankster to spring from behind the armchair. This was not thoughtful; this was baffling. I carefully placed the flowers on the counter, the ceramic vase seeming to mock my confusion. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over the contact list, a sudden, hot flush creeping up my neck. Someone had clearly made a monumental error, and I felt a profound sense of being completely disregarded.

[story 2]

My eyes scanned the wrapping paper, searching desperately for the expected inscription, the tiny piece of paper that usually held the key to any kind intention. It was gone. Just vibrant, slightly wilting roses, undeniably expensive, yet utterly devoid of context. A slow, simmering tension began to coil in my chest, a heavy, uncomfortable pressure that made my shoulders rise towards my ears. I let out a slow, controlled exhale, trying to keep my expression neutral, but the tightness around my mouth betrayed me. "Seriously?" I whispered, the sound thin and brittle in the quiet apartment. I turned the vase slowly in my hands, examining the stems as if they held the secret to a hidden message. This felt like a deliberate snub, a casual dismissal of the effort involved. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, the vibrant colors of the flowers mocking the dull, heavy feeling settling in my stomach.

[story 3]

When I finally peeled back the cellophane, the sheer volume of the blooms hit me first—a cascade of deep crimson peonies, impossibly perfect. Then, the realization dawned: nothing. No card. No signature. Just flowers, presented as if they were a mandatory tax payment. A low growl escaped my throat, startling me. I snatched the arrangement, the stems cool and slick against my palm, and took a deliberate step back. My hands clenched into tight fists at my sides; the knuckles turned white with the sudden, sharp rigidity of my control slipping. "Are you kidding me?" I said, my voice pitched low and dangerously steady. I paced three steps across the floor, my boots clicking sharply on the hardwood. Every nerve ending seemed to be screaming at the sheer audacity of the gesture. This was a profound lack of respect, a deliberate void where communication should have been. I spun around, glaring at the empty space where the sender should have been.
